sentences.text: At night.
And then, then they sent us to a block.
And the next morning, again, they took us all out of the block.
And, and besides us they had brought other people from other concentration camps-- from Buchenwald from Dachau, they all arrived more or less the same time.
We had to go again across the appelplatz and again to undress.
And then came Doctor Mengele.
And, and, and just like, like in a meat house when they look-- first grade meat, second grade meat-- they look at everybody and literally, like this-- up and down.
My father-- that's the first time that we were separated from my father.
My father was put, not to be gassed, to be worked until he died.
And my brother and I were sent to Auschwitz III.
It was called Buna.
That was a camp where the largest industrial complex of Europe was being built by 60,000 workers to produce gasoline from, from coal and Buna.
Buna was synthetic rubber because any war machine runs on parts and gasoline.
And airplanes also, they run on gasoline.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's really-- after we were separated, it was the last time we saw our father.

And after the war, I found out that my father was assigned to an arbeitskommando that again, carried huge excrement, human excrement to a place-- they were unloading in, in, in the barrels from the barracks.
And he survived exactly one week.
He was literally beaten to death.
From a man that was part of my hometown that was assigned to stay in Auschwitz, not to go to Auschwitz III, to the other, to the work kommando.
And he, for some reason, became stubendienst.
Stubendienst means in the service of the barrack.
One-- there were always two wings.
And he said he helped him because he knew him from our hometown.
But he-- He also, after he was separated from us, he lost-- we think he lost his ability to survive.
And let me tell you something.
He was only-- he was born 1890.
He was 42, 52-- at that time was old.
He was lucky that he didn't-- that even let him-- that, that, our whole transport was not sent to the gas chambers.
Normally, a transport arrived and right away, separate.
And it was civilians.

We were considered already hardened, hardened inmates, so to speak-- that, that anybody that survived the continuous four years was considered must be strong, must be efficient, knows how to survive, knows how to work, how to, to, to, to obey orders and so.
But as I said, after he was separated, he lost his will to live.
That's what he told.
I met this guy in Israel by sheer coincidence.
I didn't know, I didn't know that he survived.
His name was--
He was a stube-dienst.
He was assigned to stay in the back to clean.
And, and, and, and he returned every night.
SS, no, no.
I don't know.
Guards could have done this.
Anybody.
Anybody that was weak and couldn't work-- the weaker he was, the more they beat him, the more they, they punished him.
It was typical-- the, the, the attitude of the Nazis.
They were, I mean, sadist and barbaric.
No, no, we-- let me put it this way.
The first two weeks, the camp wasn't finished yet.
We marched out-- it took over an hour-- and marched back.
And then, the third week, some barracks were half ready.
We stayed there in the barracks.
The first weeks, we did nothing but chicanery.
I told you the story that the snow-- we had to fill up the the things.
There we did it with sand.
And who were the kapos?
All Poles.
Not Germans.
Poles.
And I, I, I said it in-- when, when I testified later in the Auschwitz trial-- one name was Oberkapo Bolek.
It was a rrrrr, beefy Pole.
He was such a murderer.
He literally-- I was told-- got awards, was praised by SS for each person that he killed.
Every day they were literally competing with each other, the kapos, who, who is a better killer than the other.
That's what was going on.
And no mercy.
No understanding.
They were such anti-Semitic bastards.
I could never believe-- to me, it was so hard to understand that a person that suffers from the Nazis and knows how beastialic, how, how, how, how inhuman they are, could do this with gusto.
With gusto!
Not because he was forced.
He, he, he was-- he wanted to become oberkapo, muchas kapo.
Oberkapo is super kapo, you know.
He was the head of all the kapos.
That's was but the beginning.
Then, we were assigned to work in the factory, together with civilians.
There were about 35,000 civilians that worked there-- Poles, forced labor from Yugoslavia, from all the occupied territories.
For example, if one was a Pole, he had a P.
If one was a Frenchman, he had a F, a sign.
There were Frenchman there, all kinds of people that were skilled laborers.
You know, locksmiths, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] carpenters, mechanics, electricians, what have you.
I-- look what I did.
I was young.
And I was cocky and, and, and sharp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After, after work we went to the barracks.
We're eating.
And then, we were not supposed to hand out that much, but for some reason, I detected-- they call this the prominent-- you remember what prominent means-- the prominenten block.

It was a block where all the people that worked in the SS officers-- somebody was a, a barber, the other guy was, was a, a typist, the other guy was a bookkeeper, the other guys worked in the-- where they give out uniforms, in the storage, what have you.
They all lived in the separate block.
They were all Germans.
There were very few Poles.
And the Lageralteste, which is, which is a very powerful person in the camp, he had sometimes by far more power than his SS sergeant.
Believe me.
And he also was manipulating the SS.
At that time was when they called us anti-social, you had a black triangle.
He wasn't a criminal.
Anti-social.
Anti-social was swindlers, people that married women and exploited them, small swindlers, insurance betrayers, all kind of people.
And this guy was from Cologne.
In Germany, each province has its own dialect.
There's the Bavaria dialect, the Rhenish dialect, a Westphalian dialect, a Saxon dialect, Bavarian dialect, everybody has Thuringian dialect.
And he talked-- he was so primitive that he couldn't even speak Hochdeutsch.
A real German.
He was talking real like a primitive-- but he was the Lageralteste.
And I saw that this man was constantly looking at his boots.
Yeah, that was also privalege.
He had, he got boots they took away from a Jewish civilian.
And, and he normally should have gone to, to Germany.
But he had connections because he was Lageralteste.
They allowed him.
He wanted boots.
He got a pair of boots.
And he had boots that were shining like a mirror.
And all the time he looked at his boots, that was his life.
Like somebody, or people, the car is his life.
He can polish the car day and night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- enough I knew about psychology.
In the camp you learn.
You have no choice, you learn fast.
And I saw him, he looked at his boots.
And then, I went to him, and I knew at that time a little bit of Rhenish slang, the accident, the, the, the accent.
And I talked to him in his Rhenish accent.
I said, you know-- and by the way, you had to address him, Herr Lageralteste.
You were a number, but he was the Herr. 'Herr' means 'senor,' 'mister.' Herr Lageralteste.
I, I was in Dachsenhausen.
I, I always glanced and polished the Stiefel, Stiefel, the, the boots of the block Lageraltest.
I'm an expert in that area.
It made an impression that I spoke his, his accent.
He said, where are you from?
I said, I'm actually from Westphalia from Gelsenkirchen.
Oh.
You know, my city was known to have one of the finest soccer teams in Germany, Schalke 04.
And I said, you know Schalke 04?
Oh, yeah of course.
The soccer team.
Yeah.
He knows all about it.
He said, really?
And I was still small.
I was only 17.
I was still small.
He said, you really?
Or are you just bullshitting me, so to speak, in German he said that.
I said, well, take your boots off.
Give me the things, and I will show you.
And that's what I did.
It took me half an hour.
And he hired me as his Stiefel putzer, as his boot.
That was my first step to protectsia, to get away from, from the normal citizens, from the normal inmate that, that in Auschwitz, at best could survive three or four months.
OK?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on, he took me-- he liked so much what I did for him-- he, he had-- by the way, the Lageraltester had a special room for himself.
Two rooms-- a sleeping room and a day room.
Right?
Vis a vis the entrance to the camp so he could see what was going on.
And, and, and he said, you know how to clean?
You know how to cook?
Of course, I said.
He took the finest food, the finest piece of meat.
He just went to the kitchen and said, I, I am Lageraltester.
I want it.
And they gave it to him because the people that worked there, the, the, the inmates were afraid.
He had power to say, you did this and this, although it was all a lie.
And then it was the end of his career.
Everybody wanted to work in the kitchen.
That was, for the speak, a visa to survive on the food.
So I didn't know how to cook, but I learned.
I asked him.
I said, I know, but isn't there something like this?
He gave me answers that I needed.
Then, one day, this guy, the Lageraltester, he had so much freedom that he stole food from the kitchen and went-- he had the right to visit the workers that worked outside, the civilians.
He was exchanging this for vodka.
And one day, they caught him really drunk like a pig.
That was the end of his career.
Was the end of his career and the, the, the green triangle-- a professional criminal.
There was-- the, the the second in command was the Lagerschreiber.
Schreiber means the official, like a bureaucrat.
He was the second in command because he had control of all the, the, the registers, the names, who worked here, who were there, who worked well, who worked bad, and, and the names.
He had a very responsible job there in, in--
Right, they call it in German.
But he was actually in charge of all the registry, the inside registry of all the documentation they have who is there, who died, and who is sick, who was deported, who was transferred.
That was his job.
It was a very responsible job as far as the SS is concerned.
They want to know at all times what is going on.
They want to know at all times that everybody is there.
And they have to have confidence in him that he is not hiding something, is not swindling because, otherwise, people could escape, and they would never know.
So they had strict control over the registration, over all the documentation about who is there, and where they-- in which block they are, how old they are, where they work, and what have you.
He took over.
And since I was already there, he kept me.

And then, I started working for the underground, mainly because just listening to the conversation that they had, and he constantly had SS men, SS-- Scharfuhrer, Oberscharfuhrer, Hauptscharfuhrer, they came to him because when they wanted to implement something in the camp, they had to go to him, to the Lageralteste.
He made things happening.
An SS, for example, who wanted to have a special selection, a special that, a special this, he went to the Lageralteste.
I heard that this block is coming and this is coming, that the-- all I hear, at 6 o'clock, at 7 o'clock, this and that and that.
So I worked not for the Lageralteste.
And unfortunately, sometimes there were-- how do you call?
There were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] orders that came from Berlin-- not enough people work.
You have too many people involved in services.
We don't want that.
Everybody goes to work.
If they want to service, they want to clean, after work let him do it.
So sometimes, I was, was, was forced for two, three weeks to work in, in the factories.
And then again, he got tired because when you work, when you clean after work, you don't do a good job.
He, he, he, I cooked for him, I made his bed, everything I did for him.
So I worked for, for, for almost two years for the Lageralteste.
And I had tremendous opportunity there to help other people.
Especially, I've always been to the kitchen in the name of the Lageralteste.
I want an extra barrel of food.
In, in 1943, they bought from [NON-ENGLISH], 40,000 grapefruits.
And they all came to us.
And there were a lot of young people there.
I would say 30% were young people.
And they were so hungry because the food was inadequate.
I mean, it was not enough to live and not enough to die.
And I for two-- I, I talked to the blockaltester, if they have youngsters, they should send them, and I gave them food.
And I went to Israel.
They recognized me.
At least 10 people came to me, you saved my life.
You know that.
Yeah, you couldn't believe it.
They recognized me.
On the world, world gathering, they all live in Haifa, most of them that survived.
Anyway, the point is then I cooked for him sometimes good stuff.
And guess where I cooked?
About a distance of 100 yards, there was a latrine.
And it was stinking like hell there.
And there was next to the latrine, was a little room where they kept all their cleaning materials.
And that's where I cooked.
[LAUGHING] Nobody-- and no one would smell this.
It's where the cooking is-- He is in trouble, I'm in trouble.
The Lageralteste is in trouble because he wasn't allowed to do that.
He did it because he, he felt he is an important person.
So that's where it didn't [INAUDIBLE].
And, and I always reported when, when there was a selection.
As I said, whatever was to be done, the SS man in charge had to discuss it with him and make the arrangement with the Lageralteste.
He was the one that really implemented all the orders that came from the SS.
So I got involved in this, but mostly in, in supplied information.
That was it.
Whenever something was going on, I let him know.
There was an active underground, you know, in Auschwitz.
Let whom know?
Hm?
There, there, there was one person-- only people who were very involved knew about it because it was top secret because, God forbid, if they knew there's an underground, they'd shoot them all.
And they'd torture everybody who'd tell them who it is.
I, I told the, the, the headquarter of the underground was in the, in the sick barracks.
In Buna, there was-- in Auschwitz III, there was a sick barrack because people worked into the factories, for the IG Farben.

And they were interested that if somebody is slightly sick, that he get-- and he had an important job to do, you worked-- you worked on a plane, you worked on a, on a, on important machinery, they had no way to replace him.
They had to hold-- if he had a key job, the whole work came to a standstill, OK?
So they, they insisted that they have a sick barrack.
And people are not-- are slightly sick to get minimal medicine to restore him as soon as possible so he can go back to work.
OK?
So there, there are a few old time Communists, the whole leadership of the Communists and socialists.
And they came from Buchenwald to Auschwitz, not from Sachsenhausen.
And there was one guy whose name was Leiser.
He was a Polish Communist.
And he was the inofficial head of the underground.
Yeah.
He was once--
Leiser.
Leiser.
Leiser Sillman.
Yeah.
He's still alive.
When I was in Warsaw with the, with the March of the Living, I visited him.
In Warsaw.
Yeah, yeah.
Sillman.
Right now, his name is-- he, he changed his name to Polish.
Starski or something.
He changed his name to Polish, not, not a Jewish name.
He married a Polish woman.
He was a big shot in Poland.
He, the, the head of the underground in Auschwitz was a Polish Communist named Cyrankiewicz that later became for 15 years the president of Poland.
And they were together under the Pilsudski regime in pre-war Poland, as Communists.
They were arrested.
They were living in Brest-Litovsk.
It was a very famous imprisonment.
I supplied information.
By listening.
I was always pretending I don't hear anything.
Then, I was in the same room all the time, was cleaning, was cleaning his shoes, was, was doing something.
And in fact, I was listening.
SS, SS people.
I said the SS fuhrers.
The SS officials.
Not guards.
Officials.
They were supposed to implement the orders that they got from the Lagerkommandant went directly to the Lageralteste.
And he was implementing it inside.
Oh, yeah.
I'll give an example.
I told him that they're bringing now hundreds of thousands of people from Hungary.
OK?
And, and, and the SS told him that most of them are being gassed right away.
And only the strongest are being distributed among the camps.
Let me tell you, Auschwitz had 34 satellite camps.
Most people don't know that.
The small camps, they worked for Heinkel, for, for aircraft factories, for this and for that.
And they're building the tanks.
And, and, and, and, and they were small camps.
Some had only 1,000.
All around Auschwitz in a radius of maybe 100 kilometers.
Gleiwitz-- they were-- in Gleiwitz alone, there was Gleiwitz I, Gleiwitz II, Gleiwitz III.
Three Gleiwitz camps were there.
They worked in factories.
So, and, and, and, and he told him, for example, that you have to prepare room because they will-- they were-- they have no place where to put them.
And they built tents, giant tents, where they had 1,000 people in a tent.
They had-- there were no barracks for them.
Not, not enough barracks because they brought them at such a speed.
You know, within three, four months, they, they, they brought 600,000 Hungarian Jews to Auschwitz.
Or, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the SS fuhrers told the Lageralteste the Gestapo, Reichfuhrer Himmler, said we have to kill more.
We have to make selections.
Anybody that is not very strong is shaky.
And there will-- on certain date, they will bring, not, not only Mengele, other doctors.
Mengele was the chief doctor.
But they will make selections.
So prepare for this.
So when, when they knew this, then there were important people that they had to save, they took them out, changed the, changed the list.
We did all kinds of manipulations to sabotage the orders of the Nazis to kill people.
And then, we knew of other people, their chances of survival are nil, they changed them.
That's what they did.
Were you still with your brother?
Yeah, my brother, look here, my brother, no, he, he was in a different barrack.
My brother became-- there was a small camp building commander that did repairs.
There were, there were masons, there were electricians, there were painters, there were carpenters.
They did all kind of things.
And there were three people that did this.
And he, he was one of them.
He was very handy, my, my brother.
And this would get him in trouble later.
And he worked in the camp.
The SS sometimes had repairs to do.
They called them to build a chimney, to do-- built an addition to a, to a house.
And so that's what he did.
Oh, oh, oh yeah, I want to talk what happened with my brother.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In late 1944, we already heard around Auschwitz shooting.
The Red Army came within 100 kilometers to Auschwitz.
And the SS got very frightened, so to speak.
And there were local partisans, guerrilla fighters, that at night attacked small places for-- small contingent of the SS order or the, the German Wehrmacht were living.
And they would start shooting.
And on top of this, the underground had information that there is a plan in case that the Red Army comes so close that there's danger that they will conquer Auschwitz, they should kill them all.
And that's what a diabolic plan they had.
All this was known to the underground.
Nobody knew about it.
Very few people knew about it.
They had a plan to use conquered, heavy artillery, Russian artillery that they conquered in the war, and, and, and, and Russian shells.
And then by artillery, shoot it, it into rubble and kill everybody.
That was the plan.
And then, to the world, blame the Russians did it.
They knew there were camps.
They knew there were prisoners, but they didn't give a damn.
They just wanted to, to, to [INAUDIBLE] And they wanted to destroy everything.
That's what they did.
No.
Let me put it this way.
All those years, there was a one, one agreement that nobody runs away with the permission of his fellow prisoners because if one ran away, in most instances, a hundred had to pay the price of this.
They were killed.
By staying all night, this guy, also, Wiseman, he had to, they had to stand there too, to watch them as they were shooting people.
But in case, that they knew, for the underground knew, that they will kill everybody, when the day comes, let's make organized, a mass outbreak.
Whoever gets saved gets saved.
And the rest, what have you.
But if they anyway will implement their plan-- but the plan, all the details, it wasn't-- this SS plan was known to SS and how they found out, the underground, I don't know.
But they found out about it.
And they knew that this is an official plan concocted by the Gestapo headquarters in Berlin.
And if they have a chance, they will do that, the Nazis.
And for some reason, they assigned those three people-- my brother and two of his friends-- one, one his name was Nathan Wiseman, and the other guy's name was Yellick Wasserman.
One was a medical student, the other was, was a law student.
And what they demanded is they should themself escape and get in touch with the partisan fighters outside, with Polish fighting groups.
And then organize because they know the inside, able to come back.
They should disappear.
Nobody should know.
They would cover up, which was very difficult to do.
But that was the plan.
In order to get out of the camp, they wanted to make a short in the electricity in the barbed wire.
And there was a Polish political prisoner, a Polish political prisoner, who was the camp electrician.
And he was part of the conspiracy.
He also wanted to get out.
In the last minute, he went and betrayed the plan to a fellow prisoner, to a Polish doctor who was an anti-semitic bastard.
And he right away went to the Lagerkommandant and told him the story.
They were arrested.
And then they were tortured for two weeks in the famous torture block in Auschwitz, in block 11.
I was, I was there just a few weeks ago, a few months ago.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And when I heard about it, I was very upset.
And, and, and I-- was very difficult, but I asked my brother, why didn't you tell me about it?
He was saying, of course I didn't tell you about it.
If you knew about it, you would be sitting there too, he told me.
He said why, why you have to get my conviction.
After that I forgot about it.
I attach good feeling because I really knew already by that time, we knew that the war is lost for the Nazis.
We heard about Stalingrad.
We knew that it's a question of time, who would have the strength and the health to survive.
That was-- it was a race this time for everybody to survive.
And here, putting his life on the line, I, I didn't know yet that they planned to hang him.
We didn't know that.
We knew that they tortured them to get more information to find out who else was involved.
Interesting enough, there were at least 10 people that knew about it.
At least 10.
They was not a big underground.
The underground was very small, maybe 15 people at most.
Nobody was arrested.
That means that they did not disclose anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, about two or three weeks passed by.
One day, people marched in.
And they built three tremendous gallows with reflectors.
They presented a show to deter other people.
To frighten because they were afraid that these things will spread.
People will get more courage.
And, and really, there will be a mess of it.
